question: Why is Principal Propagation needed for a cloud-to-on-premise integration instead of simply calling the backend with a single shared technical/service user?
hints: Think individual accountability and audit trail
What does the backend AUTHORITY-CHECK see in each case?
expectedAnswer: A shared technical user collapses every cloud user's backend activity into one identity, losing individual accountability and per-user authorization enforcement; Principal Propagation preserves the original authenticated user's identity end-to-end so the backend authorizes and logs the call against the real person.
detailedAnswer: With a technical user, the on-premise AUTHORITY-CHECK evaluates the technical user's (typically broad) authorizations regardless of which cloud user actually triggered the call, and every change document, audit log entry, and SoD analysis attributes the action to the shared account rather than the real individual. Principal Propagation instead carries the cloud-authenticated user's identity through the OAuth SAML Bearer flow into the backend, so the backend resolves a real local user and authorizes/logs against that specific person - preserving segregation of duties and audit integrity across the cloud boundary. Not every integration needs this: batch or system-to-system flows with no individual end user are legitimately served by a scoped technical user instead.
followUps: When would a scoped technical user actually be the simpler, correct architectural choice instead of Principal Propagation?
How would an auditor discover a landscape is using a shared technical user where individual accountability was actually required?
commonMistakes: Defaulting to a shared technical user purely for implementation convenience without evaluating the accountability requirement
Assuming Principal Propagation is mandatory for every cloud-to-on-premise call regardless of the use case
scenario: A new BTP-based expense approval app calls an on-premise ECC backend using one shared RFC service user, and internal audit flags that approvals can no longer be traced to the individual approver.

question: In a BTP destination configuration, what actually changes at runtime when the Authentication property is set to 'PrincipalPropagation' versus 'BasicAuthentication' or 'NoAuthentication'?
hints: Whose identity ends up on the wire?
Which one involves a certificate/assertion exchange with Cloud Connector?
expectedAnswer: BasicAuthentication sends fixed, destination-stored credentials for every call regardless of who triggered it; NoAuthentication sends none; PrincipalPropagation instead triggers the runtime to obtain and forward a certificate/assertion representing the currently logged-on cloud user's identity through Cloud Connector to the backend.
detailedAnswer: A destination with BasicAuthentication or a stored OAuth client-credentials configuration always authenticates as the same fixed identity - simple, but every call looks identical to the backend no matter which end user triggered it. NoAuthentication is used for genuinely public/unauthenticated endpoints. PrincipalPropagation is fundamentally different: at call time, the runtime obtains a client certificate (or SAML assertion, depending on configuration) bound to the currently authenticated cloud user, Cloud Connector forwards this proof of identity to the backend, and the backend resolves it to a local user before running AUTHORITY-CHECK - meaning the destination configuration alone determines whether individual identity survives the hop at all.
followUps: What backend-side configuration is still required even after a destination is set to PrincipalPropagation?
What happens if a destination is mistakenly left as BasicAuthentication for an app that actually needed per-user authorization?
commonMistakes: Assuming setting the destination's authentication type alone is sufficient without backend user mapping
Confusing PrincipalPropagation with simply passing a username as a parameter in the call payload
scenario: A developer configures a new destination and asks why setting Authentication to PrincipalPropagation doesn't work until the backend security team makes an additional change.

question: Walk through the OAuth 2.0 SAML Bearer Assertion flow end to end, and explain why this specific grant type - rather than Authorization Code or Client Credentials - is the one used to implement Principal Propagation.
hints: Who issues the SAML assertion and who consumes it?
Client Credentials authenticates what identity?
expectedAnswer: The cloud app presents a SAML assertion (issued by the trusted identity provider, typically IAS) vouching for the current user's identity to the backend's OAuth server, which validates the assertion and issues a scoped access token for that specific user; Client Credentials only authenticates the application itself with no user context, and Authorization Code requires an interactive redirect unsuitable for a server-to-server hop.
detailedAnswer: In the flow, the cloud application already holds the user's identity from its own XSUAA-issued JWT after the user's initial login. To call the on-premise backend as that user, it requests a token from the backend's OAuth authorization server, presenting a SAML assertion that the backend trusts because it trusts the issuing identity provider (IAS). The backend validates the assertion's signature and issuer, resolves the asserted identity to a local user, and issues a short-lived, narrowly scoped access token bound to that user, which the app then uses for the actual backend API call. Client Credentials grant only proves the calling application's own identity with no per-user context at all, making it appropriate for system-to-system integration but useless for propagating an individual identity. Authorization Code grant requires a live interactive browser redirect to the identity provider, which doesn't fit a background server-to-server call happening after the user has already authenticated once.
followUps: What has to be configured on the backend OAuth server before it will accept and trust this SAML assertion at all?
How short-lived is the resulting access token typically, and why does that matter for security?
commonMistakes: Assuming Client Credentials grant can somehow carry per-user identity
Not realizing the backend must explicitly trust the SAML issuer before the assertion exchange will succeed
scenario: A team building a hybrid integration debates whether Client Credentials or SAML Bearer Assertion is the right OAuth grant type for calling an on-premise backend as the logged-in user.

question: Explain Cloud Connector's system-level versus resource-level access control lists, and why a resource-level allowlist is the better security practice.
hints: System-level controls which hosts are exposed at all
Resource-level controls specific URL paths per system
expectedAnswer: System-level ACLs determine which backend hosts/systems Cloud Connector exposes to the cloud subaccount at all; resource-level ACLs additionally restrict which specific URL paths or services on that system are reachable, so allowlisting only the resources actually required (rather than the whole system) minimizes attack surface if the cloud application or its credentials are ever compromised.
detailedAnswer: System-level access control is coarse-grained: it decides whether a given on-premise host/instance is exposed through the Cloud Connector tunnel to the BTP subaccount at all. Resource-level access control is the finer-grained layer sitting beneath it, restricting the specific paths (e.g., a single OData service or RFC function group) reachable on that already-exposed system. A call to any path not explicitly allowlisted at the resource level is rejected by Cloud Connector before it ever reaches the backend, regardless of what authorization the calling user actually has there. Best practice is to always scope resource-level ACLs to only the exact paths required by the integration, because allowlisting an entire system exposes every service on it to any cloud application with connectivity - a significant, often unnecessary increase in blast radius if the cloud side is ever compromised.
followUps: How would you audit an existing Cloud Connector configuration for overly broad system-level exposure?
What error signature would you expect if a resource-level path is not allowlisted, versus a backend authorization failure?
commonMistakes: Allowlisting an entire backend system instead of specific required resource paths
Assuming resource-level restriction is optional once system-level access is granted
scenario: A security review of an existing Cloud Connector setup finds an entire ECC system allowlisted at the system level with no resource-level restrictions configured.

question: What role does Identity Authentication Service (IAS) play as the SAML assertion issuer in Principal Propagation, and what must be configured on the backend for the assertion to actually be trusted?
hints: Think trust anchor, not just identity store
What does the backend need in its trust configuration?
expectedAnswer: IAS acts as the trusted identity provider that authenticates the cloud user and issues the SAML assertion vouching for that identity; the on-premise backend must explicitly establish trust in IAS as an assertion issuer (typically via its certificate/metadata imported into the backend's trust configuration) before it will accept and validate any assertion IAS produces.
detailedAnswer: IAS is the identity provider that authenticates the cloud user during their original login and is later asked to produce a SAML assertion attesting to that identity when the SAML Bearer flow needs to prove who is calling. This assertion is only useful if the backend's OAuth server has been explicitly configured to trust IAS as a valid issuer - typically by importing IAS's signing certificate or federation metadata into the backend's trust store. Without this trust configuration step, a syntactically valid, correctly signed assertion from IAS will still be rejected by the backend simply because the backend has no reason to believe IAS is a legitimate issuer for its domain - this is a distinct, separate trust relationship from Cloud Connector's own network connectivity trust to the backend.
followUps: What happens operationally if IAS's signing certificate is rotated but the backend's trust configuration isn't updated?
How is this trust relationship different from the trust Cloud Connector itself establishes with the backend?
commonMistakes: Assuming any SAML assertion from any identity provider will be accepted without explicit backend trust configuration
Forgetting to update the backend trust store after an IAS certificate rotation
scenario: A principal-propagated call that worked for months suddenly starts failing assertion validation after an identity provider certificate renewal.

question: Explain the two genuinely distinct trust relationships involved when a principal-propagated call travels from a BTP application through Cloud Connector into an on-premise backend, and why conflating them leads to troubleshooting mistakes.
hints: One trust is about the tunnel/certificate, the other is about the asserted user identity
What fails if only one of the two is correctly configured?
expectedAnswer: Cloud Connector's connectivity trust is the backend's trust in the certificate Cloud Connector itself presents to establish the tunnel/connection; principal propagation's identity trust is the backend's separate trust in the SAML assertion issuer (IAS) vouching for the specific end user. A call can pass one and fail the other, producing very different error signatures that are easily confused.
detailedAnswer: Cloud Connector connectivity trust is essentially a network/transport-level relationship: the backend trusts the Cloud Connector instance's certificate to allow the tunnel and forward calls at all, independent of which end user is involved. Principal propagation identity trust is entirely separate: even with a perfectly functioning Cloud Connector tunnel, the backend also needs to trust the SAML assertion issuer (IAS) to accept the claimed user identity being propagated through that tunnel. A misconfigured Cloud Connector certificate causes the call to fail before any identity is even considered - a connectivity-layer rejection. A misconfigured or expired IAS trust configuration lets the tunnel succeed but causes the identity assertion itself to be rejected - a completely different failure mode with a different fix. Treating these as one trust relationship leads consultants to, for example, re-import a Cloud Connector certificate when the actual problem is an expired IAS trust entry, wasting significant troubleshooting time.
followUps: What specific error message or log entry would help you tell these two failures apart in practice?
Which of these two trust relationships would you expect to break first after a routine certificate rotation project?
commonMistakes: Treating Cloud Connector connectivity trust and principal propagation identity trust as a single configuration to fix together
Re-establishing Cloud Connector's own trust when the actual failure is in the SAML assertion trust chain
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[BTP Application] -->|Connectivity Trust: CC Certificate| B[Cloud Connector Tunnel]
  B --> C[On-Premise Backend]
  A -->|Identity Trust: SAML Assertion from IAS| D[Backend OAuth Server]
  D -->|Validates IAS as Trusted Issuer| C
  C --> E[User Mapping + AUTHORITY-CHECK]

question: Compare manually maintained backend user mapping tables versus attribute-based identity federation for resolving a propagated cloud identity to a local backend user, and explain concretely why the manual approach breaks down past a pilot rollout.
hints: Think about who has to maintain the mapping table and how often
What happens the moment a new user logs in who was never manually mapped?
expectedAnswer: A manual 1:1 mapping table requires an explicit entry per user before propagation will resolve to a local account, which works fine for a small pilot group but becomes an operational bottleneck and single point of failure at scale; attribute-based federation instead resolves the local user dynamically from a shared identifying attribute (like corporate email), scaling to any number of users with zero incremental mapping effort.
detailedAnswer: A manual mapping table ties every cloud identity to a specific local backend user record one row at a time - fine for 5-10 pilot users where a security admin manually creates each entry, but as soon as the rollout expands to hundreds or thousands of users, every single new hire, transfer, or newly onboarded user requires a corresponding manual mapping entry before principal propagation will resolve them to a local user at all. Miss the entry and the call fails with an identity-resolution error that looks like a completely broken feature to the business, even though the architecture itself is sound. Attribute-based federation instead configures the backend to resolve the local user dynamically at runtime using a shared attribute already present in both identity stores, most commonly corporate email or an employee ID - eliminating the need for any per-user mapping maintenance and scaling to the full user population automatically as long as the shared attribute is consistently populated on both sides.
followUps: What data quality prerequisite must be true across both identity stores for attribute-based federation to work reliably?
How would you migrate an existing manual mapping table population to attribute-based federation without a disruptive cutover?
commonMistakes: Assuming a manual mapping approach that worked for a pilot will scale unchanged to full rollout
Not validating that the chosen federation attribute is consistently populated and unique across both identity systems before cutover
scenario: An insurance client's BTP claims application worked flawlessly for pilot users but failed for most of the broader rollout population because the backend user mapping had only ever been manually created for the pilot accounts.

question: A propagated access token successfully reaches the backend through Cloud Connector, but the call still fails. What role can token audience/scope validation play in this failure, and how do you distinguish it from a backend AUTHORITY-CHECK failure?
hints: What is the token actually scoped to allow?
Does an audience mismatch even reach the ABAP authorization layer?
expectedAnswer: If the access token's audience or scope doesn't match what the backend OAuth resource server expects for that specific service, the call is rejected at the token-validation layer before any AUTHORITY-CHECK is ever executed - a fundamentally different failure than a properly-scoped, properly-authorized call that fails a genuine ABAP authorization check.
detailedAnswer: An OAuth access token carries an audience (intended recipient service) and a set of scopes (what operations it's authorized for at the OAuth layer) as part of the token issuance process. If the token was issued for a different audience than the service actually being called, or lacks a required scope, the backend's OAuth resource server rejects the call during token validation - this happens entirely before the request is handed off to the ABAP stack, so it never reaches AUTHORITY-CHECK at all. This produces an error signature that looks superficially similar to an authorization failure but has nothing to do with the propagated user's actual PFCG roles; the fix is correcting the OAuth client/scope configuration, not touching any role. Distinguishing this from a genuine AUTHORITY-CHECK failure requires checking the token validation/OAuth layer logs first, before assuming the propagated user simply lacks backend authorization.
followUps: Where would you look to confirm a token audience/scope mismatch versus a genuine authorization gap?
How would you design the OAuth client/scope configuration to avoid this class of failure recurring for new integrations?
commonMistakes: Assuming any post-Cloud-Connector failure must be an ABAP authorization issue and going straight to role changes
Not checking OAuth token validation logs before escalating to the authorization team
scenario: A newly onboarded integration passes Cloud Connector and reaches the backend, but every call is rejected with an error the team initially assumes is a missing authorization.

question: Draw and explain the complete end-to-end trust chain for a hybrid principal-propagated scenario, from the user's original cloud login through to the backend AUTHORITY-CHECK. Why must an architecture review cover every link, not just the cloud or on-premise half in isolation?
hints: Count the distinct hops and trust boundaries
What happens to the overall security posture if you only harden one half?
expectedAnswer: The full chain spans cloud identity authentication (IAS) to XSUAA token issuance to Cloud Connector network/ACL trust to backend principal propagation identity trust to backend classic AUTHORITY-CHECK - a genuine architecture review must validate every link, because a design that only secures the cloud or on-premise half in isolation leaves the other half as an unreviewed weak point that undermines the whole chain.
detailedAnswer: The complete trust chain is: the user authenticates to IAS, which issues identity assertions consumed by XSUAA to produce the cloud application's JWT; the cloud application then uses the SAML Bearer flow to obtain a backend-scoped access token, presenting an assertion the backend trusts because it trusts IAS as an issuer; the call itself is tunneled through Cloud Connector, which independently enforces its own network-level connectivity trust and system/resource ACLs; finally, the backend resolves the propagated identity to a local user and runs the same AUTHORITY-CHECK it always would for that user. A security architecture review that only examines PFCG roles on the backend, or only examines XSUAA scopes on the cloud side, misses the fact that any one of these five links being weak (an overly broad Cloud Connector ACL, a stale IAS trust certificate, a manual user-mapping gap) breaks the integrity of the entire chain regardless of how well the other links are designed.
followUps: Which single link in this chain, in your experience, is most often skipped during architecture reviews?
How would you present this end-to-end chain to an audit team that's used to reviewing only PFCG roles?
commonMistakes: Scoping a security review to only the cloud or only the on-premise half of the architecture
Presenting Cloud Connector ACLs and backend authorization as a single combined control rather than distinct links
diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[User Login] --> B[IAS - Identity Authentication]
  B --> C[XSUAA - JWT Issuance]
  C --> D[SAML Bearer Assertion to Backend]
  D --> E[Cloud Connector - Network + ACL Trust]
  E --> F[Backend - Principal Propagation Identity Trust]
  F --> G[Backend - User Mapping Resolution]
  G --> H[AUTHORITY-CHECK]

question: A cloud application reports it 'cannot reach' an on-premise resource. Describe your systematic approach to determining whether the root cause is a Cloud Connector ACL rejection, a principal propagation/trust failure, or a backend AUTHORITY-CHECK failure.
hints: Each failure domain has a distinct error signature and log location
Work outside-in: network/ACL first, then identity trust, then authorization
expectedAnswer: Check Cloud Connector's access control log first to rule out a system/resource ACL rejection (call never reaches the backend); if it passed, check the backend's identity resolution/trust logs for an assertion or user-mapping failure; only if identity resolution succeeded do you move to SU53/STAUTHTRACE on the backend to investigate a genuine AUTHORITY-CHECK failure.
detailedAnswer: The systematic approach follows the order calls actually traverse the chain. First, check the Cloud Connector administration UI's access control/connectivity log - a rejection here means the resource path was never allowlisted (or the tunnel itself is down), and the call never reached the backend at all, so backend-side troubleshooting is a waste of time. If Cloud Connector shows the call was forwarded successfully, next check the backend's OAuth/SAML assertion validation logs and user mapping configuration for an identity-resolution failure - this produces an error distinct from an authorization failure, typically indicating no local user could be resolved for the asserted identity, or the assertion issuer isn't trusted. Only once you've confirmed the call reached the backend and a local user was correctly resolved should you move to SU53 or a full STAUTHTRACE trace scoped to that resolved user, to investigate a genuine missing authorization. Skipping straight to backend authorization troubleshooting without first ruling out the earlier two layers is the single most common time-wasting mistake in hybrid landscape support.
followUps: What tooling or dashboard would you build to surface these three failure domains distinctly for a Tier 1 support team?
How would you train a support team unfamiliar with hybrid architectures to triage this correctly on first contact?
commonMistakes: Jumping directly to backend authorization troubleshooting (SU53/roles) without first ruling out Cloud Connector ACL and identity trust failures
Not having distinct, accessible logs for each of the three failure domains, forcing guesswork
scenario: A production incident ticket says only 'integration broken' with no further detail, and three different teams (cloud, network, and basis) each assume the problem is in someone else's domain.

question: A project team wants to implement full Principal Propagation for a new integration by default, adding several weeks of trust and mapping configuration work. How do you, as the lead, decide whether that complexity is actually justified versus a simpler scoped technical user?
hints: Tie the decision to a concrete business/audit requirement, not a default preference
Consider cost of complexity versus cost of a compliance gap
expectedAnswer: Base the decision on whether the integration genuinely requires individual user accountability and per-user authorization enforcement in the backend; if so, the multi-week trust/mapping investment is justified and should be budgeted for up front, but if the integration is a system-level batch or single-purpose flow with no individual end-user context, a scoped technical user is simpler, cheaper, and equally compliant.
detailedAnswer: As a lead, I'd push the team to answer one concrete question before committing timeline: does this integration need the backend to know and enforce authorization for the specific individual, for audit or SoD reasons, or is it fundamentally a system-to-system data flow with no meaningful individual actor? If audit/compliance genuinely requires individual accountability - which is common for anything involving financial postings, approvals, or sensitive data changes - then the multi-week investment in trust configuration and user mapping strategy is a justified, necessary cost, and I'd make sure it's budgeted and communicated as a compliance requirement rather than a nice-to-have. If the integration is a batch extraction job or a purely technical data sync with no individual end user, defaulting to full Principal Propagation adds real cost and operational complexity (ongoing mapping maintenance, additional trust relationships to monitor) with no corresponding audit benefit, and a properly scoped technical user with its own tightly restricted authorization is the right call. The mistake to avoid in either direction is applying one pattern by default without asking the question at all.
followUps: How would you handle a compliance team that insists on Principal Propagation for every integration regardless of use case, as a blanket policy?
How do you retroactively identify integrations in your landscape that used the wrong pattern for their actual requirement?
commonMistakes: Defaulting to Principal Propagation for every integration without evaluating whether individual accountability is actually required
Defaulting to technical users purely to save time on integrations that actually did need individual accountability for compliance reasons
scenario: A project timeline is at risk because the team assumed every new BTP-to-on-premise integration requires full Principal Propagation by default.

question: Design a Principal Propagation strategy for an enterprise landscape where a single BTP application must reach three different backend types: an S/4HANA system, a legacy ECC system, and a non-SAP third-party API. What architectural decisions differ across the three, and how do you keep the overall user-mapping strategy from fragmenting?
hints: Not every backend supports the same propagation mechanism
Think about a single source of truth for the mapping attribute across all three
expectedAnswer: Use SAML Bearer Assertion-based Principal Propagation through Cloud Connector for both the S/4HANA and ECC backends since both support the standard mechanism, but the non-SAP third-party API likely requires a different pattern (e.g., a custom OAuth token exchange or a scoped API key with user context passed as an explicit claim); regardless of mechanism, anchor all three on the same single federating attribute (such as corporate email) sourced from one identity system, so the mapping strategy doesn't fragment into three independently-maintained schemes.
detailedAnswer: For the S/4HANA and legacy ECC backends, both support the standard OAuth 2.0 SAML Bearer Assertion flow through Cloud Connector, so the architecture is consistent: establish IAS trust on each backend independently (since each backend's OAuth server maintains its own trust configuration), and resolve the local user via attribute-based federation on the same shared corporate identifier on both systems. The non-SAP third-party API almost certainly doesn't support SAP's specific propagation mechanism, so the architecture must adapt - commonly this means a custom OAuth token exchange pattern, or passing the propagated identity as an explicit claim/header that the third-party API's own authorization layer is built to consume, verified through whatever trust mechanism that API supports (mutual TLS, its own OAuth introspection, etc.). The critical architectural discipline is refusing to let each backend's team independently invent its own mapping table or attribute scheme - all three should resolve identity from the same single source of truth (typically the corporate identity system feeding IAS), using the same attribute, so a user's identity resolves consistently everywhere and there is exactly one place to fix data quality issues rather than three.
followUps: How would you handle a backend system that cannot support attribute-based federation at all and is stuck with manual mapping?
How would you monitor and alert across three heterogeneous propagation mechanisms with a single operational dashboard?
commonMistakes: Assuming all backend types support the same SAML Bearer Assertion mechanism without verifying third-party API capabilities
Allowing each backend team to independently design its own user mapping scheme, fragmenting the identity strategy
diagram:
flowchart TD
  U[Single Corporate Identity Source] --> IAS[IAS]
  IAS --> S4[S4HANA - SAML Bearer + Attribute Federation]
  IAS --> ECC[ECC - SAML Bearer + Attribute Federation]
  IAS --> TP[Third-Party API - Custom Token Exchange / Claim Passing]
  S4 --> M1[Local User Resolution - Shared Attribute]
  ECC --> M2[Local User Resolution - Shared Attribute]
  TP --> M3[API-Native Identity Verification]
